## Gradual Transformation and Coexistence Unlike other temples, Angkor Wat was never fully abandoned and continually hosted a mix of Hindu and Buddhist rituals, with Buddhist images gradually replacing or standing alongside Hindu ones. Its reuse was not an abrupt conversion but a gentle adaptation, reflecting Cambodia’s fluid religious boundaries and a respect for past traditions—leaving much Hindu imagery in place as Buddhist worship grew.  ...    Over time, Angkor Wat’s Buddhist identity contributed to its role as a symbol of Khmer culture and resilience,  ... title: Recycling Monuments: The Hinduism/Buddhism Switch at Angkor | CyArk
highlights: However, although it was originally built as a Hindu capital for the Khmer Empire, when the tenets of Buddhism gently permeated the region at the end of the 12th century, Angkor became a center of Buddhist worship. New monuments featured the Buddha and bodhisattvas, most notably the temple of Bayon, which was built by the Khmer ruler who embraced Buddhism King Jayavarman VII. Old monuments, such as the temple of Angkor Wat, were also subtly converted into Buddhist shrines, leaving the Hindu imagery in place.
